one_line_takeaway: Scoring 16 existing AI-security frameworks against a 193-item, largely LLM-generated MAS threat taxonomy, the paper finds even the best framework (OWASP ASI) covers only 65.3% of items and that non-determinism (mean 1.231/3) and data leakage (1.340/3) remain the most systematically unaddressed risk categories across the entire field.
key_finding: Coverage is systematically incomplete: OWASP Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) leads overall with 65.3% coverage (score>=2) and the highest share of score-3 (direct) items at 14.5%; CDAO GenAI Toolkit and ATFAA-SHIELD tie at 62.2% coverage; MITRE ATLAS reaches 52.8% (second-highest score-3 share, 9.8%); mid-tier frameworks (ENISA FAICP 50.8%, NIST AI RMF 48.7%, Google SecAI 44.0%, GAO AI 40.9%) provide governance/infra-level partial coverage; a lower tier (NSA and DoD documents, Cisco A2A, NIST GenAI, DIU RAI -- as low as 6.2% for DIU RAI) is constrained by narrow lifecycle or governance-only scope. By category, mean score across all 16 frameworks is highest for Memory Poisoning (1.578) and Workflow Architecture (1.543), and lowest for Non-Determinism (1.231) and Data Leakage (1.340); no category reaches a mean of 1.6. Per-category leaders (Table V): OWASP ASI leads Agent-Tool Coupling (2.00), Identity/Provenance (2.16), Memory Poisoning (2.12); CDAO GenAI leads Data Leakage (1.71) and Non-Determinism (1.68); MITRE ATLAS leads Trust Exploitation (2.09) and Workflow Architecture (2.06); ENISA FAICP leads Timing/Monitoring (1.83); OWASP ASI/CDAO/MITRE tie on Injection (2.14). By lifecycle phase, OWASP ASI leads design (avg 2.054, items from RATC/RIP/RWA, 56 items), CDAO GenAI leads development (avg 1.929, RMP/RTM, 28 items) and operation (avg 1.651 vs. OWASP ASI's 1.642, over RIDC/RDL/RTE/RND, 109 items); MITRE ATLAS drops to 1.532 in the operational phase, reflecting a design-time rather than runtime focus. Composite-maturity ranking: (1) OWASP ASI (composite 0.500, avg 1.798, 65.3% coverage, 28 score-3 items), (2) CDAO GenAI (composite 0.449, avg 1.668, 62.2%), (3) ATFAA-SHIELD (composite 0.445, avg 1.653, 62.2%, highest score-2 density at 114/193=59.1%). Five items receive score 1 from every one of the 16 frameworks (no coverage anywhere): RATC 10 (Efficiency Optimization/Resource Constraint Exploitation), RDL 29 (Data Leakage via MCTS Planning State), RND 25 (HTN Planning Non-Determinism), RND 26 (MCTS Planning Non-Determinism), RTE 33 (Other Multi-Agent Trust Exploitation Risks) -- all tied to advanced planning-algorithm internals (MCTS/HTN) or hardware-level resource interactions.
core_contribution: The paper (a NIST RFI 2026-00206 response by 'Crew Scaler', an org whose 501c3 status is pending) builds a taxonomy of 193 distinct multi-agent-system (MAS) security threat items across nine risk categories -- Agent-Tool Coupling (RATC, 21 items), Data Leakage (RDL, 34), Injection (RIDC, 7), Identity/Provenance (RIP, 19), Memory Poisoning (RMP, 16), Non-Determinism (RND, 34), Trust Exploitation (RTE, 34), Timing/Monitoring (RTM, 12), and Workflow Architecture (RWA, 16) -- and then quantitatively scores 16 existing AI security/governance frameworks (e.g. NIST AI RMF, MITRE ATLAS, OWASP Agentic Security Initiative, CDAO GenAI Toolkit, ATFAA-SHIELD, ENISA FAICP, DoD/NSA/DIU documents, Cisco A2A, GAO AI, Google SecAI) against every taxonomy item on a 3-point relevance scale (1=minimal, 2=moderate/indirect, 3=direct/specific). The result is the first (per the authors) empirical cross-framework coverage comparison for MAS security, plus lifecycle-phase (design/development/operation) breakdowns and identification of items no framework addresses at all.
threat_model: The paper is a framework-coverage survey, not a system paper proposing a novel attack or defense with its own formal threat model. Its own contribution is the taxonomy-construction and scoring methodology; the 193 threats it catalogs (attacker capabilities such as indirect prompt/observation injection to achieve 'policy-level RCE' via tool authority, cross-agent memory poisoning through shared vector stores, self-replicating prompt worms propagating over inter-agent messages, approval-fatigue/UI-abstraction exploitation, and non-determinism as an assurance gap) are themselves the object being surveyed/scored, largely generated via a 'generative AI-assisted threat modeling' process (an LLM was prompted to reason adversarially over a hand-authored technical knowledge base) and then checked by one NVIDIA-certified agentic-AI reviewer -- so the threats are analytic/anticipatory constructs, not confirmed exploited vulnerabilities, as the paper's own disclaimer states explicitly.
limitations: The paper's own disclaimer states: (1) the field moves fast enough that specific technical details are time-bounded and the document is explicitly an evolving/incremental arXiv series, not a final artifact; (2) the cataloged threats are 'plausible attack vectors derived from system properties and adversarial reasoning' and do NOT necessarily correspond to confirmed vulnerabilities, in-the-wild exploits, or vendor/government advisories -- no claim is made that any specific product or framework is actually vulnerable; (3) Phase 4 (the literature survey execution that would empirically validate/mature each threat item) is described as 'currently underway' -- the reported taxonomy and scoring reflect completed Phases 1-3 plus only 'early results' of Phase 4, i.e. the framework-coverage scores are based on a largely LLM-generated and single-reviewer-validated threat list rather than a fully surveyed/externally peer-validated one. Additional grounding-relevant caveats visible in the text itself: the threat taxonomy's initial ~1,700-candidate list was produced by prompting an LLM to threat-model the authors' own knowledge base, then pruned to 193 items by exactly one named domain-expert reviewer -- a single-annotator validation step with no reported inter-rater reliability; the authoring organization (Crew Scaler) is a 501(c)(3)-pending entity with a stated commercial/advisory interest in agentic-AI security, and the paper is explicitly framed as a policy submission (response to a NIST RFI) rather than a peer-reviewed conference/journal paper.

quantitative_results: 193 total threat items across 9 categories (RATC 21, RDL 34, RIDC 7, RIP 19, RMP 16, RND 34, RTE 34, RTM 12, RWA 16)
16 frameworks scored on a 3-point scale (1 minimal, 2 moderate, 3 direct/specific)
Expected minimal average score baseline = 2; no framework achieves majority coverage of any single category
OWASP ASI: 65.3% overall coverage, 14.5% score-3 items (28 items), composite maturity score 0.500, avg 1.798, design-phase avg 2.054
CDAO GenAI: 62.2% coverage, avg 1.668, composite 0.449; development-phase leader avg 1.929; operational-phase leader avg 1.651
ATFAA-SHIELD: 62.2% coverage, avg 1.653, composite 0.445, highest score-2 density (114 items, 59.1%)
MITRE ATLAS: 52.8% coverage, 9.8% score-3 items; drops to 1.532 avg in operational phase
ENISA FAICP 50.8%, NIST AI RMF 48.7%, Google SecAI 44.0%, GAO AI 40.9% coverage
DIU RAI: narrowest coverage at 6.2%; only 12 of the evaluated risk sections scored above baseline, all moderate
DoD Responsible AI Strategy: 27 of 174 categorized threat categories scored moderate; none scored strong
ENISA FAICP: 96 sections scoring at moderate level

Mean score per category (16-framework average): Non-Determinism 1.231, Data Leakage 1.340, Trust Exploitation 1.369 (cited as third-weakest), ... Workflow Architecture 1.543, Memory Poisoning 1.578 (strongest)

Table V per-category best framework/avg: Agent-Tool Coupling OWASP ASI 2.00; Data Leakage CDAO GenAI 1.71; Injection OWASP ASI/CDAO/MITRE 2.14; Identity/Provenance OWASP ASI 2.16; Memory Poisoning OWASP ASI 2.12; Non-Determinism CDAO GenAI 1.68; Trust Exploitation MITRE ATLAS 2.09; Timing/Monitoring ENISA FAICP 1.83; Workflow Architecture MITRE ATLAS 2.06
5 threat items score 1 (no coverage) across all 16 frameworks: RATC 10, RDL 29, RND 25, RND 26, RTE 33

Cited external result (not this paper's own): tool-result-parsing defense reduces Attack Success Rate to the lowest recorded in academic literature; Microsoft 'Spotlighting' datamarking reduces ASR from >50% to <2% -- both are Related Work citations, not this paper's experiments
Cited external result (Related Work): benchmark evaluations of tool-overloaded (20+ tool) single-agent configurations show tool selection error rates as high as 50%
baselines_compared: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
OWASP Agentic Security Initiative / Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
CDAO Generative AI Responsible AI Toolkit (DoD)
ENISA Multilayer Framework for AI Security (FAICP)
NSA/international 'Guidelines for Secure AI System Development'
DoD AI Cybersecurity Risk Management Tailoring Guide
DoD Responsible AI Strategy and Implementation Pathway
